Idiomatic Expressions

  • 等忘瑕不存往失 - Combines '等忘' (equally forget), '瑕' (faults or flaws), '不存' (do not keep), '往失' (past mistakes); suggests a state of forgetting past faults and mistakes.
  • 且人中臭气上薫空界四十万里 - Describes the pervasive smell of human odor that extends forty thousand miles into the sky; '空界' refers to the sky or heavens.
  • 诸天清浄无不厌之 - '诸天' means 'various heavens', '清浄' means 'pure and clean', '无不厌之' means 'all are disgusted by it'; suggests that the heavens are pure and clean, but are disgusted by human odor.
  • 但以受佛付嘱令防佛法 - '但以' means 'only because', '受佛付嘱' means 'received the Buddha's entrustment', '令防佛法' means 'to protect the Dharma'; indicates protection of the Dharma due to the Buddha's command.
  • 尚与人同止 - '尚' means 'still', '与人同止' means 'live together with humans'; suggests living among humans.
  • 韦将军三十二将之中最存防 - '韦将军' refers to a general named Wei, '三十二将' means 'thirty-two generals', '最存防' means 'most capable of defense'; indicates General Wei is the most capable of defense among the thirty-two generals.
  • 多有魔子魔女轻弄比丘 - '多有' means 'there are many', '魔子魔女' means 'demons and demonesses', '轻弄' means 'lightly tease', '比丘' means 'monks'; describes demons and demonesses teasing monks.
  • 道力微者并为惑乱 - '道力微者' means 'those with weak spiritual power', '并为惑乱' means 'are all confused and disturbed'; suggests those with weak spiritual power are confused and disturbed by demons.
  • 将军栖遑奔至应机除剪 - '将军' means 'general', '栖遑' means 'anxious and restless', '奔至' means 'rush to', '应机' means 'in response to the situation', '除剪' means 'eliminate and cut off'; describes the general rushing to eliminate demons in response to the situation.
  • 故有事至须往四王所 - '故' means 'therefore', '有事至' means 'there is an event', '须往' means 'must go to', '四王所' means 'the place of the Four Kings'; indicates that because there is an event, the general must go to the place of the Four Kings.
  • 时王见皆起为韦将军修童眞行防正法 - '时王' means 'at the time of the kings', '见皆起' means 'all stand up', '为韦将军' means 'for General Wei', '修童眞行' means 'practice pure and true conduct', '防正法' means 'protect the true Dharma'; describes the kings standing up to show respect for General Wei and his efforts to protect the Dharma.
  • 弟子性乐戒律如来一代所制毗尼并在座中听受戒法 - '弟子' means 'disciples', '性乐' means 'enjoy', '戒律' means 'precepts', '如来一代所制' means 'established by the Buddha in one generation', '毗尼' means 'Vinaya' (monastic rules), '并在座中听受戒法' means 'listen and receive the precepts in the assembly'; describes disciples enjoying the precepts established by the Buddha and listening to them in the assembly.
  • 因问律中诸隐文义无不决滞 - '因问' means 'ask about', '律中' means 'in the Vinaya', '诸隐文义' means 'various obscure meanings', '无不决滞' means 'all are resolved'; indicates that disciples ask about various obscure meanings in the Vinaya, and all are resolved.
  • 然此东华三寳素有山海水石往往多现 - '然' means 'however', '东华' means 'Eastern China', '三寳' means 'the Three Jewels', '素有' means 'have always had', '山海水石' means 'mountains, seas, water, and stones', '往往多现' means 'often appear'; suggests that in Eastern China, the Three Jewels have always had mountains, seas, water, and stones that often appear.
  • 但谓其灵而敬之 - '但' means 'but', '谓其灵' means 'consider them spiritual', '敬之' means 'revere them'; indicates that people consider these natural phenomena spiritual and revere them.
  • 顾访失由莫知投诣 - '顾访' means 'inquire', '失由' means 'lost the reason', '莫知' means 'do not know', '投诣' means 'where to go'; suggests that people do not know where to go to find the reason for these phenomena.
  • 遂因此缘随而谘请 - '遂' means 'thus', '因此缘' means 'because of this reason', '随而' means 'followingly', '谘请' means 'ask for advice'; indicates that people follow this reason and ask for advice.
  • 且沈防之相以理括之 - '且' means 'and', '沈防' means 'deep and hidden', '之相' means 'the appearance', '以理括之' means 'to summarize it with reason'; suggests that the deep and hidden appearance is summarized with reason.
  • 未曾持观不可以语也 - '未曾' means 'never', '持观' means 'hold and observe', '不可以' means 'cannot', '语也' means 'be described'; indicates that it cannot be described because it has never been observed.
  • 宣师又以感通记问天人云 - '宣师' means 'the teacher', '又以' means 'also with', '感通' means 'sensitive and responsive', '记问' means 'record and ask', '天人' means 'heavenly beings'; describes the teacher also recording and asking heavenly beings.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 王太学之大博也 - Unusual and possibly corrupt phrase; may be an honorific or title, or an error. Structure resembles a classical formula such as 'When Wang Taixue became greatly learned,' but it is unclear.
  • 周访字源出没不定 - Phrase is difficult to parse. May refer to a person named Zhou Fang (周访), whose name or origin is uncertain or variable. '字源' may refer to the origin of a character or name, but the phrase is ambiguous.
  • 有开春申君墓得其铭文皆是隶字 - Phrase suggests that when the tomb of Chunshen Jun (春申君) was opened, the inscriptions found were in clerical script (隶字). The term '开墓' is a classical expression for 'opening a tomb.'
  • 检春申是周代六国同时隶文则非吞并之日也 - Complex sentence. May be saying that the clerical script found at Chunshen Jun’s tomb was contemporary with the Warring States period (六国), not the time of unification (吞并之日).
  • 宁知迦叶佛时之事决非其耳目之所闻见也 - Rhetorical question meaning 'How could they possibly know the events of the time of the Buddha Kasyapa, which were certainly beyond their hearing or sight?'
  • 今西京城西髙四土台俗谚云是苍颉造书台 - Refers to a high earthen platform in the west of the western city (西京城), where a popular saying claims it is the site where Cangjie (仓颉) created writing.
  • 如何云隶书字古时已有 - Question meaning 'How can one say that the clerical script already existed in ancient times?'
  • 答曰苍颉于此台上增土造台观鸟迹者非无其事 - Response explaining that while Cangjie did observe bird tracks on the platform, the claim that he created writing there is not without basis.
  • 且苍颉之传此土罕知其源 - 'Moreover, the transmission of Cangjie in this land is rarely known, and his origin is obscure.'
  • 或云黄帝之臣或云古帝王也 - 'Some say he was a minister of the Yellow Emperor, others say he was an ancient king.'
  • 鸟迹之书时变一途今所絶有无益之言不劳述也 - Elliptical and possibly corrupted sentence. May mean that the writing based on bird tracks changed over time, and now such things are obsolete; it is unnecessary to speak of them.
  • 弟子是周穆王时生在初天本是迦叶佛时天为通化故周时暂现 - Complex sentence about Lu Xuanchang’s origin: 'The disciple was born during the time of King Mu of Zhou, originally a being from the initial heavens, which was the time of the Buddha Kasyapa; the heavens became unified, so he appeared temporarily in the Zhou era.'

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 謂化人者是也 - Complex structure meaning 'To say that the *huàrén* [transformed person] is the case' or 'It is said that the *huàrén* is [the one].'
  • 化人 - Term referring to a supernatural being, 'transformer,' or 'transcendent person,' often in Daoist or Buddhist contexts.
  • 示穆王云 - 'Showed to King Mu of Zhou, saying...' or 'told King Mu of Zhou that...'
  • 迦叶佛说法处 - 'The place where Buddha Kāśyapa preached.' Kāśyapa is one of the earliest historical Buddhas in Mahayana Buddhism.
  • 因造三防道塲 - 'Therefore, the *Sanfang Daochang* was built.' *Sanfang Daochang* may be a name of a temple or site.
  • 扶风获一石佛 - 'In Fufeng, a stone Buddha was obtained.' *Fufeng* is a place name, likely referring to a region or county in ancient China.
  • 弃马坊中秽汚此像 - 'Discarded in the horse yard, defiling this image.' *马坊* is a 'horse stable' or 'horse yard.'
  • 防像神瞠 - Ambiguous phrase. *防* may be a misspelling or misreading of *访* ('to visit') or *妨* ('to hinder'). *神瞠* could mean 'the deity looked angrily' or 'the deity was offended.'
  • 被责疏觉 - 'Was rebuked and woke up from the dream.' *疏觉* may be a compound meaning 'to wake up from a dream' or 'to become aware.'
  • 三牲以祭之 - 'Sacrificed three animals to it.' *三牲* refers to three types of animals used in sacrifices—usually ox, pig, and chicken.
  • 爱重物命如防一子 - 'Loves and respects all living beings as one's own child.' *防* here is likely a misspelling or misreading of *凡* ('all') or *防* ('to protect').
  • 所可祭 - 'What can be offered in sacrifice?' or 'What is appropriate for sacrifice?'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 周穆王 - King Mu of Zhou, a legendary ruler of the Western Zhou Dynasty, known for his travels and encounters with supernatural beings.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The sentence '菩萨示行怖畏手擎大石可于聚落欲下压之' is grammatically elliptical and may be read as a sequence of actions: the Bodhisattva displayed fear or power, held a large stone, and was about to drop it on the village.
  • The sentence '歘回心敬信于佛' may be read as a cause-effect sequence: the sudden turning of hearts led to faith in the Buddha.
  • The sentence '所有杀具变成莲花随处街巷华如种植瑞像方摄神力' is a run-on with multiple components: weapons turned into lotus flowers, which were planted in the streets, and the miraculous image then gathered divine power.

  • 下寿 - Natural lifespan, typically around 80 years in traditional Chinese culture.
  • 禅定 - State of deep meditation in Buddhism, indicating a peaceful and enlightened state of mind.
  • 陜中 - Region around Shaanxi province, where the narrator died.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • Long sentence must be read as two parallel clauses describing the narrator's supernatural abilities to summon food and clothing.
  • Clause must be read as a hyperbolic comparison, emphasizing the narrator's extraordinary brightness.
  • Clause must be read as a description of the narrator's ability to travel instantly by thought.
  • Clause must be read as a sequence of events: the narrator witnesses something unusual, reports it, and requests a painting.
  • Clause must be read as a sequence of observations: the narrator's ability to see through his pores, the closing of the pores after eating, and the narrator's initial state.
  • Clause must be read as a description of the narrator's full revelation of his heavenly appearance and the common belief that it was supernatural.
  • Clause must be read as a sequence of events: the time when Wu Zetian was the ruler, the report of the narrator's abilities, and the emperor's command.
  • Clause must be read as a sequence of events: the narrator's arrival, the emperor's questioning, and the emperor's hospitality.
  • Clause must be read as a sequence of events: the narrator's prolonged stay and the emperor's request for advice.
  • Clause must be read as the narrator's reluctant response and his advice to the emperor.
  • Clause must be read as a sequence of events: the emperor's acceptance of the advice, the decree to allow the narrator to perform good deeds, and the narrator's actions.
  • Clause must be read as a description of the narrator's death, indicating his peaceful and enlightened state.

  • 山路既开众僧皆罗拜一郡皆呼至圣刺史奉之如神 - Describes the mountain path being opened, the monks bowing in reverence, and the entire county calling Lǎn Cán a saint, with the governor treating him like a deity.
  • 嬾残悄然乃懐去意寺外虎豹忽尔成羣日有杀伤无由禁止 - Describes Lǎn Cán silently deciding to leave, and suddenly, a group of tigers and leopards appears outside the temple, causing daily killings that cannot be stopped.
  • 嬾残曰授我棰为尔尽驱除 - Describes Lǎn Cán asking for a stick to drive away the tigers and leopards.
  • 众皆曰大石犹可推虎豹当易制与之荆挺皆蹑而观之 - Describes the crowd saying that if Lǎn Cán could move the large stone, he could easily control the tigers and leopards, and they give him a thorny stick to watch.
  • 才出门见一虎衔之而去 - Describes Lǎn Cán just stepping out the door when a tiger snatches the stick and runs away with it.
  • 嬾残既去之后虎豹亦絶踪迹 - Describes the tigers and leopards disappearing after Lǎn Cán leaves.
  • 后李公果十年为相也 - Describes the governor, Li Gong, becoming chancellor ten years later.

[1201]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 金刚仙 - A proper name, likely the name of a Buddhist monk, possibly transliterated as 'Jingang Xian' or 'Diamond Sage' in English.

  • 居于清逺峡山寺 - Lived at Qingyuan Xia Shan Temple, where '清逺' is likely 'Qingyuan' (a place name), and '峡山寺' is 'Xia Shan Temple' (a temple in a gorge or narrow mountain pass).
  • 能梵音弹舌摇锡而咒物物无不应 - Could chant in梵音 (Sanskrit or Buddhist chanting), move his tongue, shake his锡杖 (staff), and cast spells; all things responded to his incantations.
  • 动锡杖一声召雷立震 - With one movement of his staff, he summoned thunder and caused it to strike immediately.
  • 持斧翦巨木刳而为舟 - Held an axe to cut down a large tree and hollow it out to make a boat.
  • 忽登山见一磐石上有穴 - Suddenly climbed a mountain and saw a flat rock with a hole in it.
  • 覩一大蜘蛛足广尺余 - Saw a large spider with legs over a foot wide.
  • 四虵囓卉窒其穴而去 - Four snakes bit at the grass and blocked the hole before leaving.
  • 俄闻林木有声暴猛吼骤 - Soon after, he heard a sound from the trees—sudden, fierce, and roaring.
  • 工人惧而缘木伺之 - The workers were frightened and climbed trees to watch it.
  • 果覩九首之虺长可数十丈 - Indeed, he saw a nine-headed snake (虺) that was several tens of feet long.
  • 屈曲蹙怒环其蛛穴东西其首 - It was coiled, angry, and surrounded the spider's hole, with its heads moving east and west.
  • 俄而跃西之首吸穴之卉团而飞去 - Soon, the western head jumped and sucked the grass团 (cluster) from the hole and flew away.
  • 頴脱俱尽 - Its head and body were both gone.
  • 后回东之首大划其目大呀其口吸其蜘蛛 - Later, the eastern head struck its eyes, opened its mouth wide, and sucked the spider.
  • 蜘蛛驰出以足擒穴之口翘屈毒丹然若火峻虺之咽喉去虺之目 - The spider rushed out, used its legs to seize the entrance of the hole, twisted, and the poison ball burned like fire in the snake’s throat, removing its eye.
  • 虺懵然而复苏举首又吸之蛛不见更毒虺虺遂倒于石 - The snake became dazed and revived, raised its head again to suck, but the spider was gone. It became more poisoned, and the snake fell over on the stone.

  • 毒丹 - Toxic pill or poison ball, a common element in Chinese myth and alchemy.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 兵部侍郎张均中书侍郎严挺之刑部侍郎房琯礼部侍郎韦陟常所礼谒 - Lists several high-ranking officials who often paid respects to the monk.

  • 言讫张遂潜去 - Means 'after saying this, Zhang secretly left.'

  • 及禄山之乱张均陷贼庭授伪署房琯賛两朝竟立大节 - Means 'when the An Lushan rebellion broke out, Zhang Jun fell into the hands of the rebels and was given a false title, while Fang Guan remained loyal to the two dynasties and upheld his integrity.'

  • 鼎曰菩萨得之不喜失之不悲打之不怒骂之不嗔此乃菩萨行也 - Shen Ding replied, 'A Bodhisattva does not rejoice at gain, does not grieve at loss, does not get angry when hit, and does not get angry when insulted. This is the practice of a Bodhisattva.'

  • 质甚陋 - Means 'his appearance was very plain,' describing his physical appearance.
  • 好以酒肉为食 - Means 'he liked to eat meat and drink alcohol,' indicating his dietary preferences.
  • 日衣弊裘 - Means 'he wore tattered fur coats every day,' describing his clothing.
  • 盛暑不脱 - Means 'he did not take them off even in hot weather,' emphasizing his consistent attire.
  • 蚤虮聚其上 - Means 'lice and fleas gathered on it,' highlighting the condition of his clothes.
  • 侨居孝感寺 - Means 'he lived as a guest in Xiaogan Temple,' indicating his place of residence.
  • 独止一室 - Means 'he lived alone in one room,' describing his living situation.
  • 每夕阖扉而寝 - Means 'he closed the door and slept every night,' indicating his routine.
  • 率为常矣 - Means 'this was his usual habit,' reinforcing the consistency of his behavior.
  • 性狂悖 - Means 'he had a wild and rebellious nature,' describing his personality.
  • 好屠犬 - Means 'he liked to kill dogs and cats,' indicating his violent tendencies.
  • 或醉卧道傍 - Means 'sometimes he would sleep drunk on the side of the road,' showing his erratic behavior.
  • 广陵人俱以此恶之 - Means 'the people of Guangling all hated him for this,' indicating the local reaction.
  • 有一少年以力闻 - Means 'there was a young man known for his strength,' introducing a key character.
  • 常一日少年与人对博 - Means 'one day, the young man was playing a game with someone,' setting up the conflict.
  • 大师大怒以手击其博局尽碎 - Means 'the Master became very angry and smashed the game board with his hand, breaking it completely,' describing the incident.
  • 少年曰騃儿何敢逆壮士 - Means 'the young man said, 'What a foolish child! How dare you defy a strong man!'' showing the young man's response.
  • 大师且骂而唾其面 - Means 'the Master cursed and spat in his face,' describing the Master's reaction.
  • 于是与少年鬬击而观者千数 - Means 'they then fought, and thousands of people watched,' indicating the scale of the event.
  • 少年卒不胜竟遯去 - Means 'the young man finally could not win and fled away,' showing the outcome.
  • 自是广陵人谓大师有神力 - Means 'from then on, the people of Guangling said the Master had supernatural strength,' indicating the local perception.
  • 大师亦自其力 - Means 'the Master also boasted of his strength,' showing his own attitude.
  • 往往剽夺市中金钱衣物 - Means 'he often stole money and clothes from the market,' describing his criminal activities.
  • 市人皆惮其勇莫敢拒 - Means 'the market people were all afraid of his bravery and dared not resist,' showing the fear he instilled.
  • 后有老僧召大师至曰 - Means 'later, an old monk called the Master and said,' introducing a new development.
  • 僧当死心奉戒 - Means 'a monk should die to keep the precepts,' emphasizing the importance of monastic discipline.
  • 奈何食酒 - Means 'how can you eat meat and drink alcohol,' questioning the Master's behavior.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 广陵 - Refers to Guangling, an ancient city in present-day Yangzhou, Jiangsu Province.
  • 大师 - A title often used for respected monks or scholars.
  • 孝感寺 - Refers to Xiaogan Temple, a specific temple in Guangling.
  • 博 - Refers to a type of board game, similar to chess or go.
  • 博局 - Refers to the game board used in the game.
  • 市 - Refers to the market, a central place for trade and social interaction in ancient Chinese cities.

Proper Nouns

  • 广陵 - The name of the city.
  • 大师 - The title of the main character.
  • 孝感寺 - The name of the temple.
  • 博 - The name of the game.
  • 博局 - The game board.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 肉杀犬 - Literal phrase meaning 'meat kills dogs,' likely referring to a specific event or action involving the killing of dogs for meat. Unusual and may be a metaphor or idiom.
  • 剽夺市人钱物 - Means 'robbed people in the market of their money and possessions,' indicating an act of theft.
  • 与少年同殴击 - Means 'along with young men, beat and struck,' suggesting a violent act involving multiple individuals.
  • 岂僧人之道 - Means 'Is this the way of a monk?' A rhetorical question questioning the behavior of a monk.
  • 一旦吏执以闻官 - Means 'one day, officials seized him and reported it to the authorities,' indicating a formal report to higher authorities.
  • 汝不羞人 - Means 'You do not feel ashamed,' a direct challenge to the person's sense of morality.
  • 大师怒骂曰 - Means 'The master angrily cursed and said,' introducing a direct quote from the master.
  • 蝇蚋徒嗜羶腥耳 - Means 'Flies and mosquitoes only love stench and filth,' a metaphor for lowly creatures attracted to impurity.
  • 然则吾道亦非汝所知也 - Means 'Therefore, my path is also not something you can understand,' a statement of superiority.
  • 且我清其中而混其外者 - Means 'Moreover, I am clear inside but mixed outside,' a metaphor for inner purity and outer appearance.
  • 岂若汝龊龊无大度乎 - Means 'How much more so than you, who are so petty and lack broad-mindedness?' A comparison highlighting the master's virtues.
  • 老僧卒不能屈其词 - Means 'The old monk could not be made to retract his words,' indicating the master's stubbornness.
  • 后一日大师自外来归 - Means 'The next day, the master returned from outside,' marking a change in the narrative.
  • 既入室闭户 - Means 'After entering the room and closing the door,' setting the scene.
  • 有于门隙视者 - Means 'There was someone peering through the door crack,' introducing a witness.
  • 大师坐于席有竒光自眉端发晃然照一室 - Means 'The master sat on the mat, and a strange light emanated from his eyebrows, illuminating the entire room,' a supernatural event.
  • 观者竒之具告羣僧 - Means 'The observer found it strange and told the other monks,' indicating the spread of the news.
  • 群僧来见大师眉端之光相指语曰 - Means 'The monks came to see the light from the master's eyebrows and pointed and spoke to each other,' describing the reaction.
  • 吾闻佛之眉有白毫相光 - Means 'I have heard that the Buddha's eyebrows have a white hair-like light,' a reference to Buddhist iconography.
  • 今大师有之果佛矣 - Means 'Now the master has it, so he must be a Buddha,' a conclusion drawn by the monks.
  • 遂相率而拜 - Means 'So they all bowed together,' indicating their reverence.
  • 至明日清旦羣僧俱集于庭候谒广陵大师 - Means 'By the next morning, early dawn, all the monks gathered in the courtyard to wait and greet the Great Master of Guangling,' setting the scene for the master's departure.
  • 比及开户而广陵大师已亡去矣 - Means 'By the time the door was opened, the Great Master of Guangling had already left,' indicating the master's sudden disappearance.
  • 羣僧益异其事 - Means 'The monks were even more amazed by the event,' emphasizing their reaction.
  • 因号大师为大师佛焉 - Means 'They then called the master 'Master Buddha,' naming the master in honor of his perceived divinity.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 僧人 - Refers to a Buddhist monk.
  • 吏 - Refers to government officials or officials in general.
  • 市人 - Refers to people in the market, indicating common folk.
  • 少年 - Refers to young men, often used to describe a group of younger individuals.
  • 广陵大师 - Refers to a respected Buddhist master from Guangling, a region in China.
  • 佛 - Refers to a Buddha, a central figure in Buddhism.
  • 白毫相光 - A specific feature in Buddhist iconography, where the Buddha's eyebrows are said to emit a white hair-like light, symbolizing enlightenment and divine presence.

Proper Nouns

  • 广陵大师 - The name of the respected Buddhist master.
  • 佛 - The title given to the master after his perceived divinity.
  • 白毫相光 - A specific feature in Buddhist iconography.
